sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Miscarriage is the spontaneous loss of a pregnancy before 20 weeks' gestation. Early pregnancy bleeding is common and does not always indicate pregnancy loss, but heavy bleeding, shock, severe pain, or uncertain diagnosis require urgent review.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Vaginal bleeding
- Lower abdominal pain or cramping
- Passage of tissue or clots
- Loss of pregnancy symptoms
- Hypotension or tachycardia if significant blood loss is present
- Fever or offensive discharge if septic miscarriage is suspected

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Early pregnancy bleeding does not always result in miscarriage
- Ectopic pregnancy must be considered in all sexually active patients with abdominal pain and bleeding in early pregnancy
- Prehospital diagnosis may be uncertain, especially if products of conception are not obvious
- Ask about gestation, prior ultrasound confirmation of intrauterine pregnancy, and severity of bleeding or pain

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer support and reassurance
- Perform Vital Sign Survey
- Consider oxygen if indicated
- Maintain a low threshold for calling the on-call doctor
- If tissue or fetus is passed later in gestation, preserve respectfully and transport with the patient if possible

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- ECG if indicated
- Consider IV access
- Consider point-of-care haemoglobin where available if bleeding is significant
- Consider IV fluids if clinically indicated
- Consider pain relief and antiemetic
- Consider antibiotics only in consultation with the on-call doctor if septic miscarriage is suspected
- Manage suspected sepsis as per the sepsis guideline

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Definitive diagnosis often requires ultrasound or confirmed passage of products of conception
- Retain large clots or tissue for hospital review if possible
- Miscarriage can have a profound psychological impact and care should remain sensitive and respectful
